title: Ankylosing Spondylitis
oneLiner: Chronic inflammatory axial arthritis leading to progressive spinal fusion; typically young males, HLA-B27 associated; risk of irreversible stiffness and extra-articular disease.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:** A 28-year-old male presents with insidious low back pain and stiffness for 6 months. He complains the pain is worse in the morning, lasting >30 minutes, and improves with exercise but not rest. He has difficulty bending to tie his shoes and notes his wife complained about his "hunched posture." He has had two episodes of acute red, painful eye (uveitis) in the past year. On exam, lumbar flexion is limited (Schober test <5 cm), chest expansion is <2.5 cm at the 4th intercostal space, and bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness is present.

*Dissecting the vignette:*
- **Young male:** Peak onset 20–30 years; male:female ratio ~2–3:1 (though females may be underdiagnosed)
- **Insidious onset, >3 months:** Inflammatory back pain criteria—must be gradual, not acute
- **Morning stiffness >30 min:** Key discriminator from mechanical back pain (which worsens with activity)
- **Improves with exercise, not rest:** Reverses the typical mechanical pattern; clues you into inflammatory etiology
- **Difficulty bending, hunched posture:** Early loss of lumbar lordosis; suggests axial restriction
- **Recurrent red painful eye:** Acute anterior uveitis is a classic extra-articular feature; often precedes joint symptoms
- **Limited Schober test:** Objective measure of lumbar spine mobility; normal is >5 cm increase with flexion
- **Reduced chest expansion:** Costovertebral joint involvement; normal >5 cm; <2.5 cm is significant

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Female with peripheral arthritis only** (e.g., knee, ankle) and no back pain — often misdiagnosed as seronegative RA or mechanical knee pain; look for enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia) and check HLA-B27
- **Isolated acute anterior uveitis** — ophthalmologist manages the eye, but internist must ask about back stiffness; AS is the most common underlying systemic cause of recurrent uveitis in young men
- **HLA-B27 negative but typical imaging** — about 10% of white AS patients are B27-negative; don't rule out based on genetics alone; rely on MRI sacroiliitis
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:** Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic autoimmune/autoinflammatory disease that primarily attacks the sacroiliac joints and spine, causing pain, stiffness, and eventually fusion of the vertebrae ("bamboo spine"). It can also affect the eyes, heart, lungs, and other joints.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Prevalence:** ~0.5% of the population—more common than you think; often missed in early stages
- **High stakes:** Untreated, 30% develop severe disability within 10 years; spinal fractures are life-threatening
- **Common pitfalls:** Confusing inflammatory back pain with mechanical, delaying MRI, forgetting to screen for extra-articular manifestations
- **Treatment is dramatically effective:** TNF inhibitors can halt progression and improve quality of life—early diagnosis prevents fusion
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story of How It Develops:**

The disease starts at the **enthesis** — the site where ligament, tendon, or joint capsule inserts into bone. In genetically susceptible individuals (80-90% carry HLA-B27, though this is not directly causative), an inciting trigger (possibly gut microbiome dysbiosis, subclinical intestinal inflammation, or molecular mimicry) activates a cascade of immune cells — particularly **CD8+ T cells, Th17 cells, and macrophages**. These cells produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (especially **TNF-α** and **IL-17**) that drive inflammation at entheses.

This enthesitis leads to:
- **Bone erosion** (osteoclast activation) — seen as "erosions" on MRI, often at sacroiliac joints
- **Reparative new bone formation** (osteoblast activation) — paradoxical: inflammation heals with calcification, producing **syndesmophytes** (bony outgrowths bridging vertebrae)
- **Progressive stiffening** as the spine fuses

**Why Each Key Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**
- **Morning stiffness:** Entheseal inflammation settles overnight; immobility allows inflammatory mediators to accumulate; morning movement mobilizes fluid and pain, hence improvement with exercise
- **Reduced lumbar flexion:** Syndesmophyte formation fuses the anterior longitudinal ligament and annulus fibrosus to the vertebral bodies — the spine becomes a rigid tube
- **Reduced chest expansion:** Costovertebral and costotransverse joint involvement limits rib cage excursion — patients become "chest breathers" (diaphragm compensates, but pulmonary function can drop)
- **Uveitis:** The eye is also an enthesis organ (ciliary body attachment); shared HLA-B27 immunopathology triggers acute inflammation in the anterior chamber
- **Achilles tendinitis / plantar fasciitis:** Classic enthesitis at the heel — pain with first steps in the morning, improves after walking

**Pathophysiology → Pattern Recognition Connection:**
- The **young age** (20-30 years) matches the peak of immune system activity and HLA-B27 expression
- The **insidious onset** reflects the slow accumulation of entheseal damage — not a single trauma
- The **bilateral, symmetrical sacroiliitis** is due to the mechanical stress on weight-bearing axial joints, which entheses are most prone to inflammation
- **Exercise improvement vs. rest worsening** — because motion reduces local cytokine concentration and prevents stiffness
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No identifiable trigger; strong genetic predisposition (HLA-B27, ERAP1, IL23R variants) | "No preceding infection, family history of AS in male uncle" |
| **Genetic** | HLA-B27 (present in 80-90% of cases) | "HLA-B27 positive on ancestrally European background" |
| **Post-infectious / Reactive** | *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Yersinia*, *Campylobacter*, *Salmonella* (via molecular mimicry with HLA-B27) | "Developed back pain 3 weeks after episode of bloody diarrhea / food poisoning" |
| **Associated with IBD** | Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis — subclinical gut inflammation may trigger AS | "Known Crohn's; presented with low back pain and heel pain" |
| **Psoriatic arthritis overlap** | Psoriasis shares IL-23/Th17 pathway; 5-10% develop axial disease mimicking AS | "Scaly plaques on elbows, nail pitting, plus inflammatory back pain" |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic + HLA-B27 positivity. The majority have no clear infectious trigger.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria — Modified New York Criteria (most widely used for AS):**

*Radiologic criterion (must have one):*
1. Bilateral sacroiliitis (grade ≥2) **OR**
2. Unilateral sacroiliitis (grade ≥3)

*Clinical criteria (must have one):*
1. Inflammatory back pain (≥3 of: age <40, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, night pain with morning stiffness >30 min)
2. Limited lumbar spine motion in both sagittal and frontal planes (Schober test, lumbar lateral flexion)
3. Limited chest expansion (≤2.5 cm at 4th intercostal space)

*Definite AS*: Radiologic criterion + at least 1 clinical criterion.

**Key Tests and Interpretation:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Pelvis X-ray / Sacroiliac joints** | Bone erosions, sclerosis, ankylosis of SI joints | First-line imaging; criteria for diagnosis | Grade 0 normal, 1 suspicious, 2 erosion/sclerosis, 3 partial ankylosis, 4 complete fusion. **Bilateral grade ≥2** confirms sacroiliitis |
| **MRI sacroiliac joints** | Bone marrow edema (osteitis), erosions, bony ankylosis | Early detection before X-ray changes; gold standard for "non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis" | **Active inflammation** = subchondral bone marrow edema on STIR or T2 fat-sat; erosion = loss of cortical bone |
| **HLA-B27** | Genetic marker | If present, supports diagnosis; if negative, does not rule out | **Positive in 80-90%** of white AS patients; lower in African (50%) and Japanese (poor correlation). Use as probabilistic clue |
| **ESR / CRP** | Acute phase reactants | Rule out infection; monitor disease activity | May be normal in 40%; if elevated, suggests active inflammation; **do not rely on normalcy to exclude** |
| **CBC** | Anemia of chronic disease | Screen for chronic illness | Normocytic, normochromic; may be low |
| **PFTs / Chest X-ray** | Restrictive pattern (reduced TLC) | If chest expansion <2.5 cm or dyspnea | Restrictive (not obstructive) due to costovertebral fusion; **no parenchymal disease** |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Confirms / Rules Out:**
- **Single finding that confirms:** Bilateral grade ≥2 sacroiliitis on X-ray (in the right clinical context) — diagnostic.
- **Single finding that rules out:** A normal MRI of sacroiliac joints with no bone marrow edema and no erosions, combined with no clinical signs of inflammatory back pain — essentially excludes active axial spondyloarthritis.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour)** — Not usually an emergency, but if acute uveitis:
- **Uveitis:** Urgent ophthalmology referral; topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% 1 drop q1h while awake); cycloplegic (homatropine 2% for pain relief). Do **not** start systemic steroids without ophthalmologist input.

**Ward (24-72 Hours)** — For new diagnosis or flare (inpatient often for severe pain, unable to mobilize, or complication):
- **NSAID** first line — reduces pain and stiffness; enables physiotherapy.
  - *Naproxen 500 mg PO BID with PPI (omeprazole 20 mg daily) for gastric protection.*
  - *If contraindicated: Celecoxib 200 mg PO daily (Cox-2 selective, but caution in cardiovascular disease).*
- **Physiotherapy** — daily stretching, postural correction, spinal extension exercises; **critical to prevent fixed kyphosis.** Avoid high-impact activities.
- **Smoking cessation counselling** — smoking markedly accelerates radiographic progression (OR ~3); make this a ward priority.
- **Screen for extra-articular disease:** slit-lamp if eye symptoms; cardiac auscultation for aortic regurgitation; bowel history for IBD.

**Long-Term (Outpatient) — Treat-to-Target (remission or low disease activity):**

*Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors) — mainstay for active disease despite NSAIDs:*
- *Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks* (most commonly used; also effective for uveitis and IBD)
- *Etanercept 50 mg SC weekly* (effective for axial but less for uveitis)
- *Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks* (good for IBD-associated AS)
- *Certolizumab pegol* (no Fc portion → less placental transfer; consider in pregnancy)

*IL-17 inhibitors* (if TNFi fail):
- *Secukinumab 150 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks then monthly* (also effective for psoriatic arthritis)
- *Ixekizumab* (similar)

*Conventional synthetic DMARDs*: Sulfasalazine 2-3 g/day PO may help peripheral arthritis but not axial disease. **Methotrexate is ineffective for axial AS** — do not use.

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**
- **Don't prescribe long-term oral steroids** — no proven benefit for axial disease, and they cause osteoporosis (already a risk with reduced mobility)
- **Don't immobilize** — bed rest worsens stiffness; encourage activity
- **Don't rely on NSAIDs alone if patient has active MRI inflammation** — progression continues
- **Don't miss spinal fracture** — AS patients have brittle, fused spines; a minor fall can cause extension fracture through ankylosed segment with cord compression. Get CT if any new focal pain

**Physical Activity Prescription:** "Walk 30 min daily, swim (warm pool), do back extension exercises holding each position 10 seconds x 10 reps. Avoid contact sports if spine is rigid."
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Ankylosing Spondylitis | Mechanical Low Back Pain | Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis (DISH) | Reactive Arthritis (Reiter's) | Psoriatic Arthritis (Axial) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Pain | Inflammatory: morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise | Mechanical: worse with activity, better with rest | Often minimal pain; stiffness due to ossification | Acute onset after infection; usually lower limb arthritis (asymmetric) | Varies; can be inflammatory or mixed; skin/nail changes |
| Age at onset | Young (<40) | Any age; older more common | Older (>50) | Young adults (20-40) | Any age; peak 30-50 |
| Sacroiliac involvement | Bilateral, symmetrical | None | Rare; can be unilateral | Asymmetric, often unilateral | Can be bilateral but often asymmetrical |
| Spine imaging | Syndesmophytes (thin, vertically oriented); bamboo spine | Degenerative disc disease, osteophytes (traction spurs) | Flowing ossification along anterolateral spine (right side of thoracic); preserved disc height | Asymmetric sacroiliitis, "pseudo-pseudo" | Asymmetric paramarginal syndesmophytes; "skip lesions" |
| Enthesitis | Common (heel, Achilles) | Rare | Not | Common (Achilles, plantar fascia) | Common (dactylitis, enthesitis) |
| Extra-articular | Uveitis (anterior), aortitis, restrictive lung | None | Dysphagia (cervical osteophytes) | Urethritis, conjunctivitis, oral ulcers | Psoriasis, nail pits, dactylitis |
| HLA-B27 | 80-90% positive | ~8% (general population) | Not associated | 50-75% positive | 10-30% positive (higher with axial disease) |
| Key differentiator | Bilateral symmetrical sacroiliitis + inflammatory back pain + HLA-B27 | Pain pattern; no objective inflammation on imaging; normal ESR | Flowing ossification + older age + asymptomatic or mild | Preceding infection + asymmetric arthritis + urethritis/conjunctivitis | Skin psoriasis + nail changes + dactylitis |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complications:**

1.  **Spinal Fracture** (most feared — mortality 5-10%)
    - **Presentation:** A patient with known AS falls from standing height or trips, then develops new severe midline back pain ± neurologic deficit (numbness, weakness, incontinence)
    - **Key sign:** Focal tenderness at the fracture site; **do not flex/extend the spine** (risk of displacement)
    - **Imaging:** CT spine (better for ankylosed segments than plain film); MRI if neurology
    - **What to do:** Immobilize with full spine precautions; call orthopaedic/spinal surgeon immediately; avoid collaring alone — the entire spine is fragile

2.  **Cauda Equina Syndrome** (rare, late)
    - **Presentation:** Slowly progressive bilateral sciatica, saddle anesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, weakness in L5-S1 distribution
    - **Key sign:** Loss of anal tone, urinary retention
    - **What to do:** Emergency MRI whole spine (often shows dural ectasia, posterior arch erosion); neurosurgery consult

3.  **Acute Anterior Uveitis** (30-50% of patients)
    - **Presentation:** Acute onset red, painful, photophobic eye with blurred vision
    - **Key sign:** Ciliary flush, hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber)
    - **What to do:** Urgent ophthalmology referral (same day); topical steroids and cycloplegics (atropine); **do not give systemic steroids without ophthalmology**; recurrence risk high

4.  **Aortic / Cardiac Disease** (late, in long-standing disease)
    - **Aortic insufficiency:** Diastolic murmur at left sternal border; can cause heart failure; screen by annual echocardiogram after 10 years
    - **Conduction defects:** AV block (may need pacemaker); annual ECG

5.  **Restrictive Lung Disease**
    - **Presentation:** Progressive dyspnea, reduced chest expansion
    - **Key signs:** Chest expansion <2.5 cm, PFTs show reduced TLC (restrictive), normal FEV1/FVC
    - **What to do:** Encourage deep breathing exercises; pneumococcal and influenza vaccines; avoid smoking

6.  **Amyloidosis** (rare, late complication)
    - **Presentation:** Nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria, edema), hepatosplenomegaly, diarrhea from GI amyloid
    - **Key sign:** Non-resolving proteinuria without other cause
    - **What to do:** Renal biopsy; treat underlying inflammation (TNFi may reduce AA amyloid); nephrology consult

**Late Presentation Patterns — "What Happens if Untreated":**
- **"Bamboo spine"** on X-ray — complete fusion; patient has fixed kyphosis, cannot lie flat, looks at the floor; risk of fracture with minimal trauma
- **"Cervical kyphosis"** — chin-on-chest deformity; difficulty swallowing, breathing; risk of cervical fracture
- **"Restrictive lung disease"** — patient has chronic hypoxia, pulmonary hypertension, recurrent pneumonia
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: "SACROILIITIS"** (features of axial spondyloarthropathy)
- **S** - Stiffness morning >30 min
- **A** - Age <40
- **C** - Chest expansion <2.5 cm
- **R** - Response to exercise (improves)
- **O** - Onset insidious
- **I** - Inflammatory back pain pattern
- **L** - Limited lumbar motion (Schober <5 cm)
- **I** - Iritis (acute anterior uveitis)
- **I** - Imaging evidence of sacroiliitis
- **T** - Tender entheses (Achilles, plantar)
- **I** - IBD association
- **S** - Skin (psoriasis) or family history

**Alternative: "AS" = Age <40, Stiffness morning, Sacroiliitis.**

**5 High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**
1.  **The "pain at night that wakes you up"** is a hallmark: ask "Do you get pain in bed that makes you turn over?" — this is more specific than morning stiffness.
2.  **Schober test** is the most important bedside exam: mark at the sacral dimples (PSIS level) and 10 cm above; ask patient to touch toes; measure the new distance; normal increase >5 cm; AS patients show <5 cm increase.
3.  **Chest expansion** is often forgotten: measure at the 4th intercostal space (nipple line in males); normal >5 cm; AS patients can have <2.5 cm.
4.  **Don't forget the heel:** In any young man with back pain, ask about heel pain (plantar fasciitis, Achilles tendinitis). If present, think AS.
5.  **Family history of AS, uveitis, psoriasis, or IBD** is a powerful clue — ask about it.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Mistake #1:** Ordering a lumbar spine X-ray instead of sacroiliac joint X-ray. The earliest changes are at the SI joints, not lumbar spine.
- **Mistake #2:** Relying on normal CRP/ESR to rule out AS. Up to 40% have normal acute phase reactants.
- **Mistake #3:** Prescribing methotrexate for axial symptoms. It does not work for spine disease; TNFi or IL-17i are needed.
- **Mistake #4:** Not asking about prior episodes of red eye. Recurrent unilateral uveitis = AS until proven otherwise.
- **Mistake #5:** Not checking chest expansion in a patient with back pain. This is a quick, objective, and specific sign.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action & Timeframe |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| **Spinal fracture** (trauma + new back pain) | Any trauma in a patient with known or suspected AS; new focal pain ± neurology | Immobilize, immediate CT spine. Call orthopaedic spine surgeon and senior rheumatologist. **Do not manipulate spine.** |
| **Cauda equina syndrome** | Acute loss of bladder/bowel control, saddle numbness, bilateral leg weakness | Emergency MRI (within 2 hours). Call neurosurgeon. Senior review. |
| **Acute uveitis** | Red, painful, photophobic eye | **Same-day** ophthalmology consult. If hypopyon, call senior. No systemic steroids without ophthalmologist. |
| **Aortic regurgitation with heart failure** | New diastolic murmur, breathlessness, raised JVP, pulmonary edema | Urgent echo, cardiology consult (within 24h). Consider diuretics. |
| **High cervical instability** (rare, advanced disease) | Neck pain with cervical myelopathy signs (Hoffmann's, clonus, Lhermitte's) | CT C-spine with flexion/extension views. Call neurosurgery. Avoid neck manipulation. |
| **Anaphylaxis to biologic** | During or after infusion (infliximab) | Stop infusion, call crash team, give IM epinephrine 0.5 mg (1:1000). |

**Specific Thresholds (NEWS2, Lactate, GCS):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any single red score (3)** — escalate to senior nurse or FY2+; consider sepsis or complication
- **Lactate >2.0 mmol/L** — check for sepsis, hypoperfusion, or aortic dissection; repeat within 2h
- **GCS <15 or drop of 2 points** — immediate senior review; consider spinal/cranial causes

**General Rule:** Any patient with AS who develops **new, severe, unremitting back pain** — even without reported trauma — should be assumed to have a **pathological fracture** until proven otherwise. Immobilize and image urgently. **Do not "mobilize" them.**

# Ankylosing Spondylitis

**One-liner:** Chronic inflammatory axial arthritis leading to progressive spinal fusion; typically young males, HLA-B27 associated; risk of irreversible stiffness and extra-articular disease.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:** A 28-year-old male presents with insidious low back pain and stiffness for 6 months. He complains the pain is worse in the morning, lasting >30 minutes, and improves with exercise but not rest. He has difficulty bending to tie his shoes and notes his wife complained about his "hunched posture." He has had two episodes of acute red, painful eye (uveitis) in the past year. On exam, lumbar flexion is limited (Schober test <5 cm), chest expansion is <2.5 cm at the 4th intercostal space, and bilateral sacroiliac joint tenderness is present.

*Dissecting the vignette:*
- **Young male:** Peak onset 20–30 years; male:female ratio ~2–3:1 (though females may be underdiagnosed)
- **Insidious onset, >3 months:** Inflammatory back pain criteria—must be gradual, not acute
- **Morning stiffness >30 min:** Key discriminator from mechanical back pain (which worsens with activity)
- **Improves with exercise, not rest:** Reverses the typical mechanical pattern; clues you into inflammatory etiology
- **Difficulty bending, hunched posture:** Early loss of lumbar lordosis; suggests axial restriction
- **Recurrent red painful eye:** Acute anterior uveitis is a classic extra-articular feature; often precedes joint symptoms
- **Limited Schober test:** Objective measure of lumbar spine mobility; normal is >5 cm increase with flexion
- **Reduced chest expansion:** Costovertebral joint involvement; normal >5 cm; <2.5 cm is significant

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Female with peripheral arthritis only** (e.g., knee, ankle) and no back pain — often misdiagnosed as seronegative RA or mechanical knee pain; look for enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia) and check HLA-B27
- **Isolated acute anterior uveitis** — ophthalmologist manages the eye, but internist must ask about back stiffness; AS is the most common underlying systemic cause of recurrent uveitis in young men
- **HLA-B27 negative but typical imaging** — about 10% of white AS patients are B27-negative; don't rule out based on genetics alone; rely on MRI sacroiliitis

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:** Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic autoimmune/autoinflammatory disease that primarily attacks the sacroiliac joints and spine, causing pain, stiffness, and eventually fusion of the vertebrae ("bamboo spine"). It can also affect the eyes, heart, lungs, and other joints.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Prevalence:** ~0.5% of the population—more common than you think; often missed in early stages
- **High stakes:** Untreated, 30% develop severe disability within 10 years; spinal fractures are life-threatening
- **Common pitfalls:** Confusing inflammatory back pain with mechanical, delaying MRI, forgetting to screen for extra-articular manifestations
- **Treatment is dramatically effective:** TNF inhibitors can halt progression and improve quality of life—early diagnosis prevents fusion

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story of How It Develops:**

The disease starts at the **enthesis** — the site where ligament, tendon, or joint capsule inserts into bone. In genetically susceptible individuals (80-90% carry HLA-B27, though this is not directly causative), an inciting trigger (possibly gut microbiome dysbiosis, subclinical intestinal inflammation, or molecular mimicry) activates a cascade of immune cells — particularly **CD8+ T cells, Th17 cells, and macrophages**. These cells produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (especially **TNF-α** and **IL-17**) that drive inflammation at entheses.

This enthesitis leads to:
- **Bone erosion** (osteoclast activation) — seen as "erosions" on MRI, often at sacroiliac joints
- **Reparative new bone formation** (osteoblast activation) — paradoxical: inflammation heals with calcification, producing **syndesmophytes** (bony outgrowths bridging vertebrae)
- **Progressive stiffening** as the spine fuses

**Why Each Key Sign Happens (Mechanism → Sign):**
- **Morning stiffness:** Entheseal inflammation settles overnight; immobility allows inflammatory mediators to accumulate; morning movement mobilizes fluid and pain, hence improvement with exercise
- **Reduced lumbar flexion:** Syndesmophyte formation fuses the anterior longitudinal ligament and annulus fibrosus to the vertebral bodies — the spine becomes a rigid tube
- **Reduced chest expansion:** Costovertebral and costotransverse joint involvement limits rib cage excursion — patients become "chest breathers" (diaphragm compensates, but pulmonary function can drop)
- **Uveitis:** The eye is also an enthesis organ (ciliary body attachment); shared HLA-B27 immunopathology triggers acute inflammation in the anterior chamber
- **Achilles tendinitis / plantar fasciitis:** Classic enthesitis at the heel — pain with first steps in the morning, improves after walking

**Pathophysiology → Pattern Recognition Connection:**
- The **young age** (20-30 years) matches the peak of immune system activity and HLA-B27 expression
- The **insidious onset** reflects the slow accumulation of entheseal damage — not a single trauma
- The **bilateral, symmetrical sacroiliitis** is due to the mechanical stress on weight-bearing axial joints, which entheses are most prone to inflammation
- **Exercise improvement vs. rest worsening** — because motion reduces local cytokine concentration and prevents stiffness

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific Cause / Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No identifiable trigger; strong genetic predisposition (HLA-B27, ERAP1, IL23R variants) | "No preceding infection, family history of AS in male uncle" |
| **Genetic** | HLA-B27 (present in 80-90% of cases) | "HLA-B27 positive on ancestrally European background" |
| **Post-infectious / Reactive** | *Klebsiella pneumoniae*, *Yersinia*, *Campylobacter*, *Salmonella* (via molecular mimicry with HLA-B27) | "Developed back pain 3 weeks after episode of bloody diarrhea / food poisoning" |
| **Associated with IBD** | Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis — subclinical gut inflammation may trigger AS | "Known Crohn's; presented with low back pain and heel pain" |
| **Psoriatic arthritis overlap** | Psoriasis shares IL-23/Th17 pathway; 5-10% develop axial disease mimicking AS | "Scaly plaques on elbows, nail pitting, plus inflammatory back pain" |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic + HLA-B27 positivity. The majority have no clear infectious trigger.

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria — Modified New York Criteria (most widely used for AS):**

*Radiologic criterion (must have one):*
1. Bilateral sacroiliitis (grade ≥2) **OR**
2. Unilateral sacroiliitis (grade ≥3)

*Clinical criteria (must have one):*
1. Inflammatory back pain (≥3 of: age <40, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, night pain with morning stiffness >30 min)
2. Limited lumbar spine motion in both sagittal and frontal planes (Schober test, lumbar lateral flexion)
3. Limited chest expansion (≤2.5 cm at 4th intercostal space)

*Definite AS*: Radiologic criterion + at least 1 clinical criterion.

**Key Tests and Interpretation:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Pelvis X-ray / Sacroiliac joints** | Bone erosions, sclerosis, ankylosis of SI joints | First-line imaging; criteria for diagnosis | Grade 0 normal, 1 suspicious, 2 erosion/sclerosis, 3 partial ankylosis, 4 complete fusion. **Bilateral grade ≥2** confirms sacroiliitis |
| **MRI sacroiliac joints** | Bone marrow edema (osteitis), erosions, bony ankylosis | Early detection before X-ray changes; gold standard for "non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis" | **Active inflammation** = subchondral bone marrow edema on STIR or T2 fat-sat; erosion = loss of cortical bone |
| **HLA-B27** | Genetic marker | If present, supports diagnosis; if negative, does not rule out | **Positive in 80-90%** of white AS patients; lower in African (50%) and Japanese (poor correlation). Use as probabilistic clue |
| **ESR / CRP** | Acute phase reactants | Rule out infection; monitor disease activity | May be normal in 40%; if elevated, suggests active inflammation; **do not rely on normalcy to exclude** |
| **CBC** | Anemia of chronic disease | Screen for chronic illness | Normocytic, normochromic; may be low |
| **PFTs / Chest X-ray** | Restrictive pattern (reduced TLC) | If chest expansion <2.5 cm or dyspnea | Restrictive (not obstructive) due to costovertebral fusion; **no parenchymal disease** |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Confirms / Rules Out:**
- **Single finding that confirms:** Bilateral grade ≥2 sacroiliitis on X-ray (in the right clinical context) — diagnostic.
- **Single finding that rules out:** A normal MRI of sacroiliac joints with no bone marrow edema and no erosions, combined with no clinical signs of inflammatory back pain — essentially excludes active axial spondyloarthritis.

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour)** — Not usually an emergency, but if acute uveitis:
- **Uveitis:** Urgent ophthalmology referral; topical corticosteroids (prednisolone acetate 1% 1 drop q1h while awake); cycloplegic (homatropine 2% for pain relief). Do **not** start systemic steroids without ophthalmologist input.

**Ward (24-72 Hours)** — For new diagnosis or flare (inpatient often for severe pain, unable to mobilize, or complication):
- **NSAID** first line — reduces pain and stiffness; enables physiotherapy.
  - *Naproxen 500 mg PO BID with PPI (omeprazole 20 mg daily) for gastric protection.*
  - *If contraindicated: Celecoxib 200 mg PO daily (Cox-2 selective, but caution in cardiovascular disease).*
- **Physiotherapy** — daily stretching, postural correction, spinal extension exercises; **critical to prevent fixed kyphosis.** Avoid high-impact activities.
- **Smoking cessation counselling** — smoking markedly accelerates radiographic progression (OR ~3); make this a ward priority.
- **Screen for extra-articular disease:** slit-lamp if eye symptoms; cardiac auscultation for aortic regurgitation; bowel history for IBD.

**Long-Term (Outpatient) — Treat-to-Target (remission or low disease activity):**

*Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors) — mainstay for active disease despite NSAIDs:*
- *Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks* (most commonly used; also effective for uveitis and IBD)
- *Etanercept 50 mg SC weekly* (effective for axial but less for uveitis)
- *Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then every 8 weeks* (good for IBD-associated AS)
- *Certolizumab pegol* (no Fc portion → less placental transfer; consider in pregnancy)

*IL-17 inhibitors* (if TNFi fail):
- *Secukinumab 150 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks then monthly* (also effective for psoriatic arthritis)
- *Ixekizumab* (similar)

*Conventional synthetic DMARDs*: Sulfasalazine 2-3 g/day PO may help peripheral arthritis but not axial disease. **Methotrexate is ineffective for axial AS** — do not use.

**What NOT to Do (Common Pitfalls):**
- **Don't prescribe long-term oral steroids** — no proven benefit for axial disease, and they cause osteoporosis (already a risk with reduced mobility)
- **Don't immobilize** — bed rest worsens stiffness; encourage activity
- **Don't rely on NSAIDs alone if patient has active MRI inflammation** — progression continues
- **Don't miss spinal fracture** — AS patients have brittle, fused spines; a minor fall can cause extension fracture through ankylosed segment with cord compression. Get CT if any new focal pain

**Physical Activity Prescription:** "Walk 30 min daily, swim (warm pool), do back extension exercises holding each position 10 seconds x 10 reps. Avoid contact sports if spine is rigid."

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Ankylosing Spondylitis | Mechanical Low Back Pain | Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis (DISH) | Reactive Arthritis (Reiter's) | Psoriatic Arthritis (Axial) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Pain | Inflammatory: morning stiffness >30 min, improves with exercise | Mechanical: worse with activity, better with rest | Often minimal pain; stiffness due to ossification | Acute onset after infection; usually lower limb arthritis (asymmetric) | Varies; can be inflammatory or mixed; skin/nail changes |
| Age at onset | Young (<40) | Any age; older more common | Older (>50) | Young adults (20-40) | Any age; peak 30-50 |
| Sacroiliac involvement | Bilateral, symmetrical | None | Rare; can be unilateral | Asymmetric, often unilateral | Can be bilateral but often asymmetrical |
| Spine imaging | Syndesmophytes (thin, vertically oriented); bamboo spine | Degenerative disc disease, osteophytes (traction spurs) | Flowing ossification along anterolateral spine (right side of thoracic); preserved disc height | Asymmetric sacroiliitis, "pseudo-pseudo" | Asymmetric paramarginal syndesmophytes; "skip lesions" |
| Enthesitis | Common (heel, Achilles) | Rare | Not | Common (Achilles, plantar fascia) | Common (dactylitis, enthesitis) |
| Extra-articular | Uveitis (anterior), aortitis, restrictive lung | None | Dysphagia (cervical osteophytes) | Urethritis, conjunctivitis, oral ulcers | Psoriasis, nail pits, dactylitis |
| HLA-B27 | 80-90% positive | ~8% (general population) | Not associated | 50-75% positive | 10-30% positive (higher with axial disease) |
| Key differentiator | Bilateral symmetrical sacroiliitis + inflammatory back pain + HLA-B27 | Pain pattern; no objective inflammation on imaging; normal ESR | Flowing ossification + older age + asymptomatic or mild | Preceding infection + asymmetric arthritis + urethritis/conjunctivitis | Skin psoriasis + nail changes + dactylitis |

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major Complications:**

1.  **Spinal Fracture** (most feared — mortality 5-10%)
    - **Presentation:** A patient with known AS falls from standing height or trips, then develops new severe midline back pain ± neurologic deficit (numbness, weakness, incontinence)
    - **Key sign:** Focal tenderness at the fracture site; **do not flex/extend the spine** (risk of displacement)
    - **Imaging:** CT spine (better for ankylosed segments than plain film); MRI if neurology
    - **What to do:** Immobilize with full spine precautions; call orthopaedic/spinal surgeon immediately; avoid collaring alone — the entire spine is fragile

2.  **Cauda Equina Syndrome** (rare, late)
    - **Presentation:** Slowly progressive bilateral sciatica, saddle anesthesia, bladder/bowel dysfunction, weakness in L5-S1 distribution
    - **Key sign:** Loss of anal tone, urinary retention
    - **What to do:** Emergency MRI whole spine (often shows dural ectasia, posterior arch erosion); neurosurgery consult

3.  **Acute Anterior Uveitis** (30-50% of patients)
    - **Presentation:** Acute onset red, painful, photophobic eye with blurred vision
    - **Key sign:** Ciliary flush, hypopyon (pus in anterior chamber)
    - **What to do:** Urgent ophthalmology referral (same day); topical steroids and cycloplegics (atropine); **do not give systemic steroids without ophthalmology**; recurrence risk high

4.  **Aortic / Cardiac Disease** (late, in long-standing disease)
    - **Aortic insufficiency:** Diastolic murmur at left sternal border; can cause heart failure; screen by annual echocardiogram after 10 years
    - **Conduction defects:** AV block (may need pacemaker); annual ECG

5.  **Restrictive Lung Disease**
    - **Presentation:** Progressive dyspnea, reduced chest expansion
    - **Key signs:** Chest expansion <2.5 cm, PFTs show reduced TLC (restrictive), normal FEV1/FVC
    - **What to do:** Encourage deep breathing exercises; pneumococcal and influenza vaccines; avoid smoking

6.  **Amyloidosis** (rare, late complication)
    - **Presentation:** Nephrotic syndrome (proteinuria, edema), hepatosplenomegaly, diarrhea from GI amyloid
    - **Key sign:** Non-resolving proteinuria without other cause
    - **What to do:** Renal biopsy; treat underlying inflammation (TNFi may reduce AA amyloid); nephrology consult

**Late Presentation Patterns — "What Happens if Untreated":**
- **"Bamboo spine"** on X-ray — complete fusion; patient has fixed kyphosis, cannot lie flat, looks at the floor; risk of fracture with minimal trauma
- **"Cervical kyphosis"** — chin-on-chest deformity; difficulty swallowing, breathing; risk of cervical fracture
- **"Restrictive lung disease"** — patient has chronic hypoxia, pulmonary hypertension, recurrent pneumonia

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: "SACROILIITIS"** (features of axial spondyloarthropathy)
- **S** - Stiffness morning >30 min
- **A** - Age <40
- **C** - Chest expansion <2.5 cm
- **R** - Response to exercise (improves)
- **O** - Onset insidious
- **I** - Inflammatory back pain pattern
- **L** - Limited lumbar motion (Schober <5 cm)
- **I** - Iritis (acute anterior uveitis)
- **I** - Imaging evidence of sacroiliitis
- **T** - Tender entheses (Achilles, plantar)
- **I** - IBD association
- **S** - Skin (psoriasis) or family history

**Alternative: "AS" = Age <40, Stiffness morning, Sacroiliitis.**

**5 High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**
1.  **The "pain at night that wakes you up"** is a hallmark: ask "Do you get pain in bed that makes you turn over?" — this is more specific than morning stiffness.
2.  **Schober test** is the most important bedside exam: mark at the sacral dimples (PSIS level) and 10 cm above; ask patient to touch toes; measure the new distance; normal increase >5 cm; AS patients show <5 cm increase.
3.  **Chest expansion** is often forgotten: measure at the 4th intercostal space (nipple line in males); normal >5 cm; AS patients can have <2.5 cm.
4.  **Don't forget the heel:** In any young man with back pain, ask about heel pain (plantar fasciitis, Achilles tendinitis). If present, think AS.
5.  **Family history of AS, uveitis, psoriasis, or IBD** is a powerful clue — ask about it.

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Mistake #1:** Ordering a lumbar spine X-ray instead of sacroiliac joint X-ray. The earliest changes are at the SI joints, not lumbar spine.
- **Mistake #2:** Relying on normal CRP/ESR to rule out AS. Up to 40% have normal acute phase reactants.
- **Mistake #3:** Prescribing methotrexate for axial symptoms. It does not work for spine disease; TNFi or IL-17i are needed.
- **Mistake #4:** Not asking about prior episodes of red eye. Recurrent unilateral uveitis = AS until proven otherwise.
- **Mistake #5:** Not checking chest expansion in a patient with back pain. This is a quick, objective, and specific sign.

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ICU / Crash Team:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action & Timeframe |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| **Spinal fracture** (trauma + new back pain) | Any trauma in a patient with known or suspected AS; new focal pain ± neurology | Immobilize, immediate CT spine. Call orthopaedic spine surgeon and senior rheumatologist. **Do not manipulate spine.** |
| **Cauda equina syndrome** | Acute loss of bladder/bowel control, saddle numbness, bilateral leg weakness | Emergency MRI (within 2 hours). Call neurosurgeon. Senior review. |
| **Acute uveitis** | Red, painful, photophobic eye | **Same-day** ophthalmology consult. If hypopyon, call senior. No systemic steroids without ophthalmologist. |
| **Aortic regurgitation with heart failure** | New diastolic murmur, breathlessness, raised JVP, pulmonary edema | Urgent echo, cardiology consult (within 24h). Consider diuretics. |
| **High cervical instability** (rare, advanced disease) | Neck pain with cervical myelopathy signs (Hoffmann's, clonus, Lhermitte's) | CT C-spine with flexion/extension views. Call neurosurgery. Avoid neck manipulation. |
| **Anaphylaxis to biologic** | During or after infusion (infliximab) | Stop infusion, call crash team, give IM epinephrine 0.5 mg (1:1000). |

**Specific Thresholds (NEWS2, Lactate, GCS):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any single red score (3)** — escalate to senior nurse or FY2+; consider sepsis or complication
- **Lactate >2.0 mmol/L** — check for sepsis, hypoperfusion, or aortic dissection; repeat within 2h
- **GCS <15 or drop of 2 points** — immediate senior review; consider spinal/cranial causes

**General Rule:** Any patient with AS who develops **new, severe, unremitting back pain** — even without reported trauma — should be assumed to have a **pathological fracture** until proven otherwise. Immobilize and image urgently. **Do not "mobilize" them.**
